facts: Hyundai Motor Group plans to break ground on a new electric vehicle factory near the Georgia coast.
The proposed plant site is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The total investment for the new EV facility is $5.5 billion.
President Joe Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) into law last month, complicating Hyundai's manufacturing plans.
Prior to the IRA's signing, electric vehicles from Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis qualified for federal tax credits of up to $7,500.
In May, President Biden publicly praised Hyundai executives for selecting the Bryan County site for the future plant.
Under the newly signed IRA, EVs assembled overseas no longer qualify for the $7,500 tax credit.
The Hyundai Ioniq5 and Kia EV6 are currently assembled in South Korea and thus lose eligibility for the price reduction under the new law.
To qualify for the $7,500 tax credit, pickups, SUVs, and vans must be priced at less than $80,000.
All other vehicle types must cost less than $55,000 to qualify for the tax credit.
Buyers must have an annual income of less than $150,000 to be eligible for the tax credit.
Final assembly of qualifying vehicles must take place in the United States, Canada, or Mexico.
Battery minerals and other components cannot be sourced from "foreign entities of concern," which explicitly include China and Russia.
The IRA is designed to discourage overseas production and force automakers to build domestic supply chains for the North American market.
The law aims to spur more domestic production of EVs while shifting supply chains away from foreign adversaries like China and Russia.
Hyundai's reliance on overseas assembly for models like the Ioniq5 and EV6 conflicts with the IRA's domestic assembly requirement.
The article notes that the tax credit structure is intended to compel automakers to establish domestic manufacturing and supply chain operations.
source_title: Inflation Reduction Act a wrench in Hyundai plans for Georgia electric vehicle plant
